revision.text: #REDIRECT [[Omi_province
#REDIRECT [[Omi Province]]

''For the geographical term that refers to the ancient Japanese province of Omi, see'' [[Omi Province]].
'''Omi''' (臣). The [[kabane]] that, along with [[muraji]], was reserved for the most powerful clans during the [[Yamato period]] of [[Japanese history]]. The omi clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the [[Soga]] (蘇我), the Kazuraki (葛城), the Heguri (平群) and the Kose (巨勢). By tradition these clans were considered branches of the imperial line (皇別氏族, ''kōbetsu shizoku''), and they traced their ancestry back to [[Emperor Kogen of Japan|Emperor Kōgen]], although there is no historical evidence to support this. When the kabane system was reformed into the [[eight kabane system]] in [[684]], the omi had generally fallen out of favor, and it was dropped to sixth in rank out of eight.

''For the geographical term that refers to the ancient Japanese province of Omi, see'' [[Omi Province]].
'''Omi''' (臣). Omi was an ancient [[Japan|Japanese]] hereditary title denoting rank and political standing (a [[kabane]]) that, along with [[muraji]], was reserved for the most powerful clans during the [[Yamato period]]. The omi clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the [[Soga]] (蘇我), the Kazuraki (葛城), the Heguri (平群) and the Kose (巨勢). By tradition these clans were considered branches of the imperial line (皇別氏族, ''kōbetsu shizoku''), and they traced their ancestry back to [[Emperor Kogen of Japan|Emperor Kōgen]], although there is no historical evidence to support this. When the kabane system was reformed into the [[eight kabane system]] in [[684]], the omi had generally fallen out of favor, and it was dropped to sixth in rank out of eight.

''For the geographical term that refers to the ancient Japanese province of Omi, see'' [[Omi Province]].
'''Omi''' (臣). Omi was an ancient [[Japan|Japanese]] hereditary title denoting rank and political standing (a [[kabane]]) that, along with [[muraji]], was reserved for the most powerful clans during the [[Yamato period]]. The omi clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the [[Soga]] (蘇我), the Kazuraki (葛城), the Heguri (平群), the Kose (巨勢), the Kasuga (春日) and the Izumo (出雲). By tradition those who held the kabane of omi were considered branches of the imperial line (皇別氏族, ''kōbetsu shizoku''), and they claimed that they were descendants of [[Emperor Kogen of Japan|Emperor Kōgen]], although there is no historical evidence to support this.</br>
The most powerful among the central omi added the prefix ''Ō'' (大) to omi and was referred to as Ōomi (大臣). Examples of Ōomi mentioned in the [[Nihon Shoki]] included Kazuraki no Tsubura (葛城円) during the reign of [[Emperor Richu of Japan|Emperor Richū]], Heguri no Matori (平群馬鳥) during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yuryaku of Japan|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei of Japan|Seinei]], Kose no Ohito (許勢男人) during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai of Japan|Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries, [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].</br>
When the kabane system was reformed into the [[eight kabane system]] in [[684]], the powerful omi of the time were given the kabane of [[Ason]], which ranked second under the new system, and omi was dropped to the rank of sixth.
[[ja:臣]]

''For the geographical term that refers to the ancient Japanese province of Omi, see'' [[Omi Province]].

When the kabane system was reformed into the [[eight kabane system]] in [[684]], the powerful omi of the time were given the kabane of [[ason]], which ranked second under the new system, and omi was dropped to sixth in rank.

''For the geographical term that refers to the ancient Japanese province of Omi, see'' [[Omi Province]].

Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included Kazuraki no Tsubura (葛城円) during the reign of [[Emperor Richu of Japan|Emperor Richū]], Heguri no Matori (平群馬鳥) during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yuryaku of Japan|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei of Japan|Seinei]], Kose no Ohito (許勢男人) during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai of Japan|Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].</br>
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[eight ''kabane'']] system in [[684]], the powerful ''omi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, and ''omi'' itself was dropped to sixth in rank.

Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included Kazuraki no Tsubura (葛城円) during the reign of [[Emperor Richu|Emperor Richū]], Heguri no Matori (平群馬鳥) during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yuryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], Kose no Ohito (許勢男人) during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].</br>
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[eight kabane|eight ''kabane'']] system in [[684]], the powerful ''omi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, and ''omi'' itself was dropped to sixth in rank.

'''Omi''' (臣). ''Omi'' was an ancient [[Japan|Japanese]] hereditary title denoting rank and political standing (a ''[[kabane]]'') that, along with ''[[muraji]]'', was reserved for the most powerful clans during the [[Yamato period]]. The ''omi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the [[Soga]] (蘇我), the [[Kazuraki]] (葛城), the [[Heguri]] (平群), the [[Kose]] (巨勢), the [[Kasuga]] (春日) and the [[Izumo]] (出雲). By tradition those who held the ''kabane'' of ''omi'' were considered branches of the imperial line (皇別氏族, ''kōbetsu shizoku''), and they claimed that they were descendants of [[Emperor Kōgen]], although there is no historical evidence to support this.</br>
The most powerful ''omi'' added the prefix ''Ō'' (大) to ''omi'' and was referred to as ''Ōomi'' (大臣). Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included Kazuraki no Tsubura (葛城円) during the reign of [[Emperor Richu|Emperor Richū]], Heguri no Matori (平群馬鳥) during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], Kose no Ohito (許勢男人) during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga]]s who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].</br>
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in [[684]], the powerful ''omi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''ason'', which ranked second under the new system, and ''omi'' itself was dropped to sixth in rank.
[[Category:Ancient Japan]]
[[fr:Omi (titre de noblesse)]] [[ja:臣]]

revision.comment: Robot - Removing category Japanese names per [[WP:CFD|CFD]] at [[Wikipedia:Categories for discussion/Log/2009 May 28]].
revision.text:
{{unreferenced|date=October 2008}} :''For the geographical term that refers to the ancient Japanese province of Omi, see'' [[Ōmi Province]]. ''For other meanings, see'' [[Omi (disambiguation)]].
{{nihongo|'''Omi'''|臣}} was an ancient [[Japan|Japanese]] hereditary title denoting rank and political standing (a ''[[kabane]]'') that, along with ''[[muraji]]'', was reserved for the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. The ''omi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the [[Soga]] (蘇我), the [[Kazuraki]] (葛城), the [[Heguri]] (平群), the [[Kose, Japan|Kose]] (巨勢), the [[Kasuga]] (春日) and the [[Izumo]] (出雲). By tradition those who held the ''kabane'' of ''omi'' were considered branches of the imperial line (皇別氏族, ''kōbetsu shizoku''), and they claimed that they were descendants of [[Emperor Kōgen]], although there is no historical evidence to support this.</br>
The most powerful ''omi'' added the prefix ''Ō'' (大) to ''omi'' and was referred to as ''Ōomi'' (大臣). Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included Kazuraki no Tsubura (葛城円) during the reign of [[Emperor Richu|Emperor Richū]], Heguri no Matori (平群馬鳥) during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], Kose no Ohito (許勢男人) during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].</br>
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in [[684]], the powerful ''omi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, and ''omi'' itself was dropped to sixth in rank.
{{Japan-hist-stub}}
[[Category:Ancient Japan]]
[[fr:Omi (titre de noblesse)]] [[ja:臣]] [[simple:Omi]] [[uk:Омі (титул)]]

{{about|ancient Japanese hereditary title denoting rank and political standing||Omi (disambiguation)}} {{unreferenced|date=October 2008}} {{nihongo|'''Omi'''|臣}} was an ancient [[Japan|Japanese]] hereditary title denoting rank and political standing (a ''[[kabane]]'') that, along with ''[[muraji]]'', was reserved for the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. The ''omi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the [[Soga]] (蘇我), the [[Kazuraki]] (葛城), the [[Heguri]] (平群), the [[Kose, Japan|Kose]] (巨勢), the [[Kasuga]] (春日) and the [[Izumo]] (出雲). By tradition those who held the ''kabane'' of ''omi'' were considered branches of the imperial line (皇別氏族, ''kōbetsu shizoku''), and they claimed that they were descendants of [[Emperor Kōgen]], although there is no historical evidence to support this.</br>
The most powerful ''omi'' added the prefix ''Ō'' (大) to ''omi'' and was referred to as ''Ōomi'' (大臣). Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included Kazuraki no Tsubura (葛城円) during the reign of [[Emperor Richu|Emperor Richū]], Heguri no Matori (平群馬鳥) during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], Kose no Ohito (許勢男人) during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].</br>
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684, the powerful ''omi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, and ''omi'' itself was dropped to sixth in rank. {{Portal|Ancient Japan}} {{Japan-hist-stub}}

{{about|ancient Japanese hereditary title denoting rank and political standing||Omi (disambiguation)}} {{unreferenced|date=October 2008}} {{nihongo|'''Omi'''|臣}} was an ancient [[Japan|Japanese]] hereditary title denoting rank and political standing (a ''[[kabane]]'') that, along with ''[[muraji]]'', was reserved for the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. The ''omi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the [[Soga]] (蘇我), the [[Kazuraki]] (葛城), the [[Heguri]] (平群), the [[Kose, Japan|Kose]] (巨勢), the [[Kasuga]] (春日) and the [[Izumo]] (出雲). By tradition those who held the ''kabane'' of ''omi'' were considered branches of the imperial line (皇別氏族, ''kōbetsu shizoku''), and they claimed that they were descendants of [[Emperor Kōgen]], although there is no historical evidence to support this.</br>
The most powerful ''omi'' added the prefix ''Ō'' (大) to ''omi'' and was referred to as ''Ōomi'' (大臣). Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included Kazuraki no Tsubura (葛城円) during the reign of [[Emperor Richu|Emperor Richū]], Heguri no Matori (平群馬鳥) during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], Kose no Ohito (許勢男人) during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].</br>
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684, the powerful ''omi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which ranked third under the new system, and ''omi'' itself was dropped to sixth in rank. {{Portal|Ancient Japan}} {{Japan-hist-stub}}

The ''omi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the [[Soga]] (蘇我), the [[Kazuraki]] (葛城), the [[Heguri]] (平群), the [[Kose, Japan|Kose]] (巨勢), the [[Kasuga]] (春日) and the [[Izumo]] (出雲). When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684, the powerful ''omi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which ranked third (it's actually second under the new system, and ''omi'' itself was dropped to sixth in rank. {{Portal|Ancient Japan}} {{Japan-hist-stub}}

By tradition those who held the ''kabane'' of ''omi'' were considered branches of the imperial line (皇別氏族, ''kōbetsu shizoku''), and they claimed that they were descendants of [[Emperor Kōgen]], although there is no historical evidence to support this.</br>

revision.text:
{{about|the ancient Japanese hereditary title denoting rank and political standing||Omi (disambiguation)}} {{unreferenced|date=October 2008}}
{{nihongo|'''''Omi'''''|臣|lead=yes}}, sometimes written as {{Nihongo|'''''Ohomi'''''|使主}}, was an ancient [[Japan]]ese hereditary title denoting rank and political standing (a ''[[kabane]]'') that, along with ''[[muraji]]'', was reserved for the head of the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. The ''omi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Soga clan|Soga]]|蘇我}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]]|葛城}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Heguri]]|平群}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kose, Japan|Kose]]|巨勢}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga]]|春日}}, and the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo, Shimane|Izumo]]|出雲}}, thus making them regional chieftains in their own right. By tradition, those who held the ''kabane'' of ''omi'' were considered {{Nihongo|branches of the imperial line|皇別氏族|kōbetsu shizoku}}, and they claimed that they were descendants of [[Emperor Kōgen]], although there is no historical evidence to support this.
The most powerful ''omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the ''omi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|ōomi}}. Examples of ''ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of [[Emperor Richū]], {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].
This title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the Ōkimi (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
These same characters ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in [[Daijō-daijin]], [[Sadaijin]], [[Udaijin]], [[Naidaijin]], etc.
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful ''omi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, and ''omi'' itself was dropped to sixth in rank.
==See also== {{Portal|Ancient Japan}} * [[Gōzoku]]
[[Category:Ancient Japan]] [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Titles]]
{{Japan-hist-stub}}

{{about|the ancient Japanese hereditary title denoting rank and political standing||Omi (disambiguation)}} {{unreferenced|date=October 2008}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{nihongo|'''''Omi'''''|臣|lead=yes}}, sometimes written as {{Nihongo|'''''Ohomi'''''|使主}}, was an ancient [[Japan]]ese hereditary title denoting rank and political standing (a ''[[kabane]]'') that, along with ''[[muraji]]'', was reserved for the head of the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. The ''omi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Soga clan|Soga]]|蘇我}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]]|葛城}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Heguri]]|平群}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kose, Japan|Kose]]|巨勢}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga]]|春日}}, and the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo, Shimane|Izumo]]|出雲}}, thus making them regional chieftains in their own right. By tradition, those who held the ''kabane'' of ''omi'' were considered {{Nihongo|branches of the imperial line|皇別氏族|kōbetsu shizoku}}, and they claimed that they were descendants of [[Emperor Kōgen]], although there is no historical evidence to support this.
The most powerful ''omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the ''omi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|ōomi}}. Examples of ''ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of [[Emperor Richū]], {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].
This title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the Ōkimi (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
These same characters ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in [[Daijō-daijin]], [[Sadaijin]], [[Udaijin]], [[Naidaijin]], etc.
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful ''omi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, and ''omi'' itself was dropped to sixth in rank.
== List of Ōomi == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–654)
==See also== {{Portal|Ancient Japan}} * [[Gōzoku]]
[[Category:Ancient Japan]] [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Titles]]
{{Japan-hist-stub}}

Examples of ''ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of [[Emperor Richū]], {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].
This title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the Ōkimi (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power. OMi is also an Indian name
These same characters ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in [[Daijō-daijin]], [[Sadaijin]], [[Udaijin]], [[Naidaijin]], etc.
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful ''omi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, and ''omi'' itself was dropped to sixth in rank.
== List of Ōomi == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
==See also== {{Portal|Ancient Japan}} * [[Gōzoku]]

{{about|the ancient Japanese hereditary title denoting rank and political standing||Omi (disambiguation)}} {{unreferenced|date=October 2008}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{nihongo|'''''Omi'''''|臣|lead=yes}}, sometimes written as {{Nihongo|'''''Ohomi'''''|使主}}, was an ancient [[Japan]]ese hereditary title denoting rank and political standing (a ''[[kabane]]'') that, along with ''[[muraji]]'', was reserved for the head of the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. The ''omi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Soga clan|Soga]]|蘇我}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]]|葛城}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Heguri]]|平群}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kose, Japan|Kose]]|巨勢}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga]]|春日}}, and the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo, Shimane|Izumo]]|出雲}}, thus making them regional chieftains in their own right. By tradition, those who held the ''kabane'' of ''omi'' were considered {{Nihongo|branches of the imperial line|皇別氏族|kōbetsu shizoku}}, and they claimed that they were descendants of [[Emperor Kōgen]], although there is no historical evidence to support this.
The most powerful ''omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the ''omi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|ōomi}}. Examples of ''ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of [[Emperor Richū]], {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].
This title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the Ōkimi (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
These same characters ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in [[Daijō-daijin]], [[Sadaijin]], [[Udaijin]], [[Naidaijin]], etc.
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful ''omi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, and ''omi'' itself was dropped to sixth in rank.
== List of Ōomi == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
==See also== {{Portal|Ancient Japan}} * [[Gōzoku]]

== In computer science == * [https://github.com/Tencent/omi Omi(JavaScript library)], Next generation web framework in 4kb JavaScript, from [[Tencent]] * [http://omijs.org omijs.org], Main concepts of omi framework * [https://github.com/Tencent/omi/blob/master/tutorial/omi-mvvm.md Omi MVVM]
== In rank science == {{about|the ancient Japanese hereditary title denoting rank and political standing||Omi (disambiguation)}} {{unreferenced|date=October 2008}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{nihongo|'''''Omi'''''|臣|lead=yes}}, sometimes written as {{Nihongo|'''''Ohomi'''''|使主}}, was an ancient [[Japan]]ese hereditary title denoting rank and political standing (a ''[[kabane]]'') that, along with ''[[muraji]]'', was reserved for the head of the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. The ''omi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Soga clan|Soga]]|蘇我}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]]|葛城}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Heguri]]|平群}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kose, Japan|Kose]]|巨勢}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga]]|春日}}, and the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo, Shimane|Izumo]]|出雲}}, thus making them regional chieftains in their own right. By tradition, those who held the ''kabane'' of ''omi'' were considered {{Nihongo|branches of the imperial line|皇別氏族|kōbetsu shizoku}}, and they claimed that they were descendants of [[Emperor Kōgen]], although there is no historical evidence to support this.
The most powerful ''omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the ''omi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|ōomi}}. Examples of ''ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of [[Emperor Richū]], {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].
This title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the Ōkimi (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
These same characters ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in [[Daijō-daijin]], [[Sadaijin]], [[Udaijin]], [[Naidaijin]], etc.
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful ''omi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, and ''omi'' itself was dropped to sixth in rank.
== List of Ōomi == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
==See also== {{Portal|Ancient Japan}} * [[Gōzoku]]
[[Category:Ancient Japan]] [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Titles]]
{{Japan-hist-stub}}

{{about|the ancient Japanese hereditary title denoting rank and political standing||Omi (disambiguation)}} {{unreferenced|date=October 2008}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{nihongo|'''''Omi'''''|臣|lead=yes}}, sometimes written as {{Nihongo|'''''Ohomi'''''|使主}}, was an ancient [[Japan]]ese hereditary title denoting rank and political standing (a ''[[kabane]]'') that, along with ''[[muraji]]'', was reserved for the head of the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful ''omi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, and ''omi'' itself was dropped to sixth in rank.

{{about|the ancient Japanese hereditary title denoting rank and political standing||Omi (disambiguation)}} {{unreferenced|date=October 2008}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{nihongo|'''''Omi'''''|臣|lead=yes}}, sometimes written as {{Nihongo|'''''Ohomi'''''|使主}}, was an ancient [[Japan]]ese hereditary title denoting rank and political standing (a ''[[kabane]]'') that, along with ''[[muraji]]'', was reserved for the head of the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. The ''omi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Soga clan|Soga]]|蘇我}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]]|葛城}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Heguri]]|平群}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kose, Japan|Kose]]|巨勢}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga]]|春日}}, and the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo, Shimane|Izumo]]|出雲}}, thus making them regional chieftains in their own right. All Ohomi above except Izumo (associates of [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] [[Izumo-taisha]]) and Soga (Korea) are local to present day Nara Prefecture. By tradition, those who held the ''kabane'' of ''omi'' were considered {{Nihongo|branches of the imperial line|皇別氏族|kōbetsu shizoku}}, and they claimed (hundreds years later) that they were descendants of [[Emperor Kōgen]] (himself legendary), although there is no historical evidence to support this.
The most powerful ''omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the ''omi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|ōomi}}. Examples of ''ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of [[Emperor Richū]], {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].
This title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the Ōkimi (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
The title also held a superior power in HvH game.
These same characters ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in [[Daijō-daijin]], [[Sadaijin]], [[Udaijin]], [[Naidaijin]], etc.

{{about|the ancient Japanese hereditary title denoting rank and political standing||Omi (disambiguation)}} {{unreferenced|date=October 2008}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}}{{nihongo|'''''Omi'''''|臣|lead=yes}}, sometimes written as {{Nihongo|'''''Ohomi'''''|使主}}, was an ancient [[Japan]]ese hereditary title denoting rank and political standing (a ''[[kabane]]'') that, along with ''[[muraji]]'', was reserved for the head of the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. The ''omi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Soga clan|Soga]]|蘇我}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]]|葛城}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Heguri]]|平群}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kose, Japan|Kose]]|巨勢}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga]]|春日}}, and the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo, Shimane|Izumo]]|出雲}}, thus making them regional chieftains in their own right. All Ohomi above except Izumo (associates of [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] [[Izumo-taisha]]) and Soga (Korea) are local to present day Nara Prefecture. By tradition, those who held the ''kabane'' of ''omi'' were considered {{Nihongo|branches of the imperial line|皇別氏族|kōbetsu shizoku}}, and they claimed (hundreds years later) that they were descendants of [[Emperor Kōgen]] (himself legendary), although there is no historical evidence to support this.
The most powerful ''omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the ''omi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|ōomi}}. Examples of ''ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of [[Emperor Richū]], {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].
This title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the Ōkimi (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
The name "'''Omi'''" also holds an inferior power in "HvH" (Deagleus is the HvH King alongside Omi)
These same characters ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in [[Daijō-daijin]], [[Sadaijin]], [[Udaijin]], [[Naidaijin]], etc.
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful ''omi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, and ''omi'' itself was dropped to sixth in rank.
== List of Ōomi == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
==See also== {{Portal|Ancient Japan}} * [[Gōzoku]]
[[Category:Ancient Japan]] [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Titles]]
{{Japan-hist-stub}}

{{about|the ancient Japanese hereditary title denoting rank and political standing||Omi (disambiguation)}} {{unreferenced|date=October 2008}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}}{{nihongo|'''''Omi'''''|臣|lead=yes}}, sometimes written as {{Nihongo|'''''Ohomi'''''|使主}}, was an ancient [[Japan]]ese hereditary title denoting rank and political standing (a ''[[kabane]]'') that, along with ''[[muraji]]'', was reserved for the head of the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. The ''omi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Soga clan|Soga]]|蘇我}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]]|葛城}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Heguri]]|平群}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kose, Japan|Kose]]|巨勢}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga]]|春日}}, and the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo, Shimane|Izumo]]|出雲}}, thus making them regional chieftains in their own right. All Ohomi above except Izumo (associates of [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] [[Izumo-taisha]]) and Soga (Korea) were local to [[Yamato Province|Yamato]] (present day [[Nara Prefecture]]). By tradition, those who held the ''kabane'' of ''omi'' were considered {{Nihongo|branches of the imperial line|皇別氏族|kōbetsu shizoku}}, and they claimed (hundreds years later) that they were descendants of [[Emperor Kōgen]] (himself legendary), although there is no historical evidence to support this.
The most powerful ''omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the ''omi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|ōomi}}. Examples of ''ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of [[Emperor Richū]], {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].
This title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the Ōkimi (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
The name "'''Omi'''" also holds an superior power in "HvH".
These same characters ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in [[Daijō-daijin]], [[Sadaijin]], [[Udaijin]], [[Naidaijin]], etc.
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful ''omi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, and ''omi'' itself was dropped to sixth in rank.
== List of Ōomi == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)

revision.comment: Added referenced content, changed citations needed template. Please add a clarification for "Omi" in "HvH".
revision.text:
{{about|the ancient Japanese hereditary title denoting rank and political standing||Omi (disambiguation)}} {{More citations needed|date=March 2021}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}}{{nihongo|'''''Omi'''''|臣|lead=yes}} is a hereditary [[Nobility|noble]] title (''[[kabane]]'') of ancient [[Japan]]. It was given to the descendants of the [[Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Family]] before [[Emperor Kōgen]]. Along with ''[[Muraji]]'', ''Omi'' was reserved for the head of the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. When the [[Yamato period|Yamato court]] was established, the most influential families bearing these two titles were given the title ''Ōomi'' and ''Ōmuraji'', respectively.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=Frank|title=Britannica International Encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-Britannica|year=1995|at=Omi|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
== History == The O''mi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Soga clan|Soga]]|蘇我}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]]|葛城}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Heguri]]|平群}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kose, Japan|Kose]]|巨勢}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga]]|春日}}, and the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo, Shimane|Izumo]]|出雲}}, thus making them regional chieftains in their own right.
The most powerful ''Omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the O''mi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|Ōomi}}. Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of [[Emperor Richū]], {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful O''mi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[Ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, and O''mi'' itself was dropped to sixth in rank.
== Name == The title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the ''Ōkimi'' (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
These same characters of ''Ōomi'' ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in ''[[Daijō-daijin]]'', ''[[Sadaijin]]'', ''[[Udaijin]]'', ''[[Naidaijin]]'', etc.
== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify}}{{Citation needed}}
==See also== {{Portal|Ancient Japan}} * [[Gōzoku]]
[[Category:Ancient Japan]] [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Titles]]
{{Japan-hist-stub}}
==References==

{{about|the ancient Japanese hereditary title denoting rank and political standing||Omi (disambiguation)}} {{More citations needed|date=March 2021}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}}{{nihongo|'''''Omi'''''|臣|lead=yes}} is a hereditary [[Nobility|noble]] title (''[[kabane]]'') of ancient [[Japan]]. It was given to the descendants of the [[Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Family]] before [[Emperor Kōgen]]. Along with ''[[Muraji]]'', ''Omi'' was reserved for the head of the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. When the [[Yamato period|Yamato court]] was established, the most influential families bearing these two titles were given the title ''Ōomi'' and ''Ōmuraji'', respectively.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=Frank|title=Britannica International Encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-Britannica|year=1995|at=Omi|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
== History == The O''mi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Soga clan|Soga]]|蘇我}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]]|葛城}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Heguri]]|平群}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kose, Japan|Kose]]|巨勢}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga]]|春日}}, and the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo, Shimane|Izumo]]|出雲}}, thus making them regional chieftains in their own right.
The most powerful ''Omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the O''mi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|Ōomi}}. Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of [[Emperor Richū]], {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful O''mi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[Ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, and O''mi'' itself was dropped to sixth in rank.
== Name == The title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the ''Ōkimi'' (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
These same characters of ''Ōomi'' ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in ''[[Daijō-daijin]]'', ''[[Sadaijin]]'', ''[[Udaijin]]'', ''[[Naidaijin]]'', etc.
== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}
==See also== {{Portal|Ancient Japan}} * [[Gōzoku]]
[[Category:Ancient Japan]] [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Titles]]
{{Japan-hist-stub}}
==References==

The most powerful ''Omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the O''mi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|Ōomi}}. Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of [[Emperor Richū]], {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful O''mi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[Ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, and O''mi'' itself was dropped to sixth in rank.
== Name == The title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the ''Ōkimi'' (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
These same characters of ''Ōomi'' ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in ''[[Daijō-daijin]]'', ''[[Sadaijin]]'', ''[[Udaijin]]'', ''[[Naidaijin]]'', etc.
== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}

The most powerful ''Omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the O''mi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|Ōomi}}. Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of [[Emperor Richū]], {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful O''mi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[Ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, and O''mi'' itself was dropped to sixth in rank.
== Name == The title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the ''Ōkimi'' (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
These same characters of ''Ōomi'' ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in ''[[Daijō-daijin]]'', ''[[Sadaijin]]'', ''[[Udaijin]]'', ''[[Naidaijin]]'', etc.
== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}
==See also== {{Portal|Ancient Japan}} * [[Gōzoku]]

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Japanese history stubs]] to [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]][[en:{{subst:FULLPAGENAME}}]][[category:dd]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Short description|Hereditary noble title in ancient Japan}} {{More citations needed|date=March 2021}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}}{{nihongo|'''''Omi'''''|臣|lead=yes}} is a hereditary [[Nobility|noble]] title (''[[kabane]]'') of ancient [[Japan]]. It was given to the descendants of the [[Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Family]] before [[Emperor Kōgen]]. Along with ''[[Muraji]]'', ''Omi'' was reserved for the head of the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. When the [[Yamato period|Yamato court]] was established, the most influential families bearing these two titles were given the title ''Ōomi'' and ''Ōmuraji'', respectively.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=Frank|title=Britannica International Encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-Britannica|year=1995|at=Omi|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
== History == The O''mi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Soga clan|Soga]]|蘇我}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]]|葛城}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Heguri]]|平群}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kose, Japan|Kose]]|巨勢}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga]]|春日}}, and the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo, Shimane|Izumo]]|出雲}}, thus making them regional chieftains in their own right.
The most powerful ''Omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the O''mi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|Ōomi}}. Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of [[Emperor Richū]], {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful O''mi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[Ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, and O''mi'' itself was dropped to sixth in rank.
== Name == The title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the ''Ōkimi'' (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
These same characters of ''Ōomi'' ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in ''[[Daijō-daijin]]'', ''[[Sadaijin]]'', ''[[Udaijin]]'', ''[[Naidaijin]]'', etc.
== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}
==See also== {{Portal|Ancient Japan}} * [[Gōzoku]]
==References== {{Reflist}}

{{Japan-hist-stub}}

{{Short description|Hereditary noble title in ancient Japan}} {{More citations needed|date=March 2021}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}}{{nihongo|'''''Omi'''''|臣|lead=yes}} is a hereditary [[Nobility|noble]] title (''[[kabane]]'') of ancient [[Japan]]. It was given to the descendants of the [[Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Family]] before [[Emperor Kōgen]]. Along with ''[[Muraji]]'', ''Omi'' was reserved for the head of the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. When the [[Yamato period|Yamato court]] was established, the most influential families bearing these two titles were given the title ''Ōomi'' and ''Ōmuraji'', respectively.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=Frank|title=Britannica International Encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-Britannica|year=1995|at=Omi|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
== History == The O''mi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Soga clan|Soga]]|蘇我}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]]|葛城}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Heguri]]|平群}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kose, Japan|Kose]]|巨勢}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga]]|春日}}, and the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo, Shimane|Izumo]]|出雲}}, thus making them regional chieftains in their own right.
The most powerful ''Omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the O''mi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|Ōomi}}. Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of [[Emperor Richū]], {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful O''mi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[Ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, and O''mi'' itself was dropped to sixth in rank.
== Name == The title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the ''Ōkimi'' (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
These same characters of ''Ōomi'' ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in ''[[Daijō-daijin]]'', ''[[Sadaijin]]'', ''[[Udaijin]]'', ''[[Naidaijin]]'', etc.
== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}
==See also== {{Portal|Ancient Japan}} * [[Gōzoku]]
==References== {{Reflist}}

== History == The O''mi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Soga clan|Soga]]|蘇我}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]]|葛城}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Heguri]]|平群}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kose, Japan|Kose]]|巨勢}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga]]|春日}}, and the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo, Shimane|Izumo]]|出雲}}, thus making them regional chieftains in their own right.
The most powerful ''Omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the O''mi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|Ōomi}}. Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of [[Emperor Richū]], {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful O''mi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[Ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, and O''mi'' itself was dropped to sixth in rank.
== Name == The title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the ''Ōkimi'' (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
These same characters of ''Ōomi'' ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in ''[[Daijō-daijin]]'', ''[[Sadaijin]]'', ''[[Udaijin]]'', ''[[Naidaijin]]'', etc.
== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}
==See also== {{Portal|Ancient Japan}} * [[Gōzoku]]
==References== {{Reflist}}

When the [[Yamato period|Yamato court]] was established, the most influential families bearing these two titles were given the title ''Ōomi'' and ''Ōmuraji'', respectively.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=Frank|title=Britannica International Encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-Britannica|year=1995|at=Omi|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
== History == The O''mi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Soga clan|Soga]]|蘇我}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]]|葛城}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Heguri]]|平群}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kose, Japan|Kose]]|巨勢}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga]]|春日}}, and the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo, Shimane|Izumo]]|出雲}}, thus making them regional chieftains in their own right.
The most powerful ''Omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the O''mi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|Ōomi}}. Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of [[Emperor Richū]], {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful O''mi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[Ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, and O''mi'' itself was dropped to sixth in rank.
== Name == The title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the ''Ōkimi'' (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
These same characters of ''Ōomi'' ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in ''[[Daijō-daijin]]'', ''[[Sadaijin]]'', ''[[Udaijin]]'', ''[[Naidaijin]]'', etc.
== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}

== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}

{{Short description|Hereditary noble title in ancient Japan}} {{More citations needed|date=March 2021}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}}{{nihongo|'''''Omi'''''|臣|lead=yes}} is a hereditary [[Nobility|noble]] title (''[[kabane]]'') of ancient [[Japan]]. It was given to the descendants of the [[Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Family]] before [[Emperor Kōgen]]. Along with ''[[Muraji]]'', ''Omi'' was reserved for the head of the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. When the [[Yamato period|Yamato court]] was established, the most influential families bearing these two titles were given the title ''Ōomi'' and ''Ōmuraji'', respectively.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=Frank|title=Britannica International Encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-Britannica|year=1995|at=Omi|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
== History == The O''mi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Soga clan|Soga]]|蘇我}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]]|葛城}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Heguri]]|平群}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kose, Japan|Kose]]|巨勢}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga]]|春日}}, and the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo, Shimane|Izumo]]|出雲}}, thus making them regional chieftains in their own right.
The most powerful ''Omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the O''mi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|Ōomi}}. Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of [[Emperor Richū]], {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful O''mi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[Ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, and O''mi'' itself was dropped to sixth in rank.
== Name == The title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the ''Ōkimi'' (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
These same characters of ''Ōomi'' ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in ''[[Daijō-daijin]]'', ''[[Sadaijin]]'', ''[[Udaijin]]'', ''[[Naidaijin]]'', etc.
== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}

== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}

{{Short description|Hereditary noble title in ancient Japan}} {{More citations needed|date=March 2021}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}}{{nihongo|'''''Omi'''''|臣|lead=yes}} is a hereditary [[Nobility|noble]] title (''[[kabane]]'') of ancient [[Japan]]. It was given to the descendants of the [[Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Family]] before [[Emperor Kōgen]]. Along with ''[[Muraji]]'', ''Omi'' was reserved for the head of the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. When the [[Yamato period|Yamato court]] was established, the most influential families bearing these two titles were given the title ''Ōomi'' and ''Ōmuraji'', respectively.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=Frank|title=Britannica International Encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-Britannica|year=1995|at=Omi|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
== History == The O''mi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Soga clan|Soga]]|蘇我}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]]|葛城}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Heguri]]|平群}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kose, Japan|Kose]]|巨勢}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga]]|春日}}, and the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo, Shimane|Izumo]]|出雲}}, thus making them regional chieftains in their own right.
The most powerful ''Omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the O''mi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|Ōomi}}. Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of [[Emperor Richū]], {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful O''mi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[Ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, and O''mi'' itself was dropped to sixth in rank.
== Name == The title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the ''Ōkimi'' (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
These same characters of ''Ōomi'' ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in ''[[Daijō-daijin]]'', ''[[Sadaijin]]'', ''[[Udaijin]]'', ''[[Naidaijin]]'', etc.
== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}

These same characters of ''Ōomi'' ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in ''[[Daijō-daijin]]'', ''[[Sadaijin]]'', ''[[Udaijin]]'', ''[[Naidaijin]]'', etc.
== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}
==See also== {{Portal|Ancient Japan}} * [[Gōzoku]]

{{Short description|Hereditary noble title in ancient Japan}} {{More citations needed|date=March 2021}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}}{{nihongo|'''''Omi'''''|臣|lead=yes}} is a hereditary [[Nobility|noble]] title (''[[kabane]]'') of ancient [[Japan]]. It was given to the descendants of the [[Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Family]] before [[Emperor Kōgen]]. Along with ''[[Muraji]]'', ''Omi'' was reserved for the head of the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. When the [[Yamato period|Yamato court]] was established, the most influential families bearing these two titles were given the title ''Ōomi'' and ''Ōmuraji'', respectively.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=Frank|title=Britannica International Encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-Britannica|year=1995|at=Omi|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
== History == The O''mi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Soga clan|Soga]]|蘇我}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]]|葛城}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Heguri]]|平群}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kose, Japan|Kose]]|巨勢}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga]]|春日}}, and the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo, Shimane|Izumo]]|出雲}}, thus making them regional chieftains in their own right.
The most powerful ''Omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the O''mi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|Ōomi}}. Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of [[Emperor Richū]], {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful O''mi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[Ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, and O''mi'' itself was dropped to sixth in rank.
== Name == The title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the ''Ōkimi'' (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
These same characters of ''Ōomi'' ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in ''[[Daijō-daijin]]'', ''[[Sadaijin]]'', ''[[Udaijin]]'', ''[[Naidaijin]]'', etc.
== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}

When the [[Yamato period|Yamato court]] was established, the most influential families bearing these two titles were given the title ''Ōomi'' and ''Ōmuraji'', respectively.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=Frank|title=Britannica International Encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-Britannica|year=1995|at=Omi|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
== History == The O''mi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Soga clan|Soga]]|蘇我}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]]|葛城}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Heguri]]|平群}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kose, Japan|Kose]]|巨勢}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga]]|春日}}, and the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo, Shimane|Izumo]]|出雲}}, thus making them regional chieftains in their own right.
The most powerful ''Omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the O''mi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|Ōomi}}. Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of [[Emperor Richū]], {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful O''mi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[Ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, and O''mi'' itself was dropped to sixth in rank.
== Name == The title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the ''Ōkimi'' (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
These same characters of ''Ōomi'' ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in ''[[Daijō-daijin]]'', ''[[Sadaijin]]'', ''[[Udaijin]]'', ''[[Naidaijin]]'', etc.
== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}

{{Short description|Hereditary noble title in ancient Japan}} {{More citations needed|date=March 2021}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}}{{nihongo|'''''Omi'''''|臣|lead=yes}} is a hereditary [[Nobility|noble]] title (''[[kabane]]'') of ancient [[Japan]]. It was given to the descendants of the [[Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Family]] before [[Emperor Kōgen]]. Along with ''[[Muraji]]'', ''Omi'' was reserved for the head of the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. When the [[Yamato period|Yamato court]] was established, the most influential families bearing these two titles were given the title ''Ōomi'' and ''Ōmuraji'', respectively.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=Frank|title=Britannica International Encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-Britannica|year=1995|at=Omi|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
== History == The O''mi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Soga clan|Soga]]|蘇我}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]]|葛城}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Heguri]]|平群}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kose, Japan|Kose]]|巨勢}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga]]|春日}}, and the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo, Shimane|Izumo]]|出雲}}, thus making them regional chieftains in their own right.
The most powerful ''Omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the O''mi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|Ōomi}}. Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of [[Emperor Richū]], {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful O''mi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[Ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, and O''mi'' itself was dropped to sixth in rank.
== Name == The title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the ''Ōkimi'' (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
These same characters of ''Ōomi'' ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in ''[[Daijō-daijin]]'', ''[[Sadaijin]]'', ''[[Udaijin]]'', ''[[Naidaijin]]'', etc.
== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}
