{{Short description|Hereditary noble title in ancient Japan}} {{More citations needed|date=March 2021}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}}{{nihongo|'''''Omi'''''|臣|lead=yes}} is a hereditary [[Nobility|noble]] title (''[[kabane]]'') of ancient [[Japan]]. It was given to the descendants of the [[Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Family]] before [[Emperor Kōgen]]. Along with ''[[Muraji]]'', ''Omi'' was reserved for the head of the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. When the [[Yamato period|Yamato court]] was established, the most influential families bearing these two titles were given the title ''Ōomi'' and ''Ōmuraji'', respectively.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=Frank|title=Britannica International Encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-Britannica|year=1995|at=Omi|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
== History == The O''mi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Soga clan|Soga]]|蘇我}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]]|葛城}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Heguri]]|平群}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kose, Japan|Kose]]|巨勢}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga]]|春日}}, and the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo, Shimane|Izumo]]|出雲}}, thus making them regional chieftains in their own right.
The most powerful ''Omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the O''mi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|Ōomi}}. Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of [[Emperor Richū]], {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful O''mi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[Ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, and O''mi'' itself was dropped to sixth in rank.
== Name == The title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the ''Ōkimi'' (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
These same characters of ''Ōomi'' ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in ''[[Daijō-daijin]]'', ''[[Sadaijin]]'', ''[[Udaijin]]'', ''[[Naidaijin]]'', etc.
== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}
==See also== {{Portal|Ancient Japan}} * [[Gōzoku]]

== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}

{{Short description|Hereditary noble title in ancient Japan}} {{More citations needed|date=March 2021}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}}{{nihongo|'''''Omi'''''|臣|lead=yes}} is a hereditary [[Nobility|noble]] title (''[[kabane]]'') of ancient [[Japan]]. It was given to the descendants of the [[Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Family]] before [[Emperor Kōgen]]. Along with ''[[Muraji]]'', ''Omi'' was reserved for the head of the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. When the [[Yamato period|Yamato court]] was established, the most influential families bearing these two titles were given the title ''Ōomi'' and ''Ōmuraji'', respectively.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=Frank|title=Britannica International Encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-Britannica|year=1995|at=Omi|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
== History == The O''mi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Soga clan|Soga]]|蘇我}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]]|葛城}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Heguri]]|平群}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kose, Japan|Kose]]|巨勢}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga]]|春日}}, and the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo, Shimane|Izumo]]|出雲}}, thus making them regional chieftains in their own right.
The most powerful ''Omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the O''mi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|Ōomi}}. Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of [[Emperor Richū]], {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful O''mi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[Ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, and O''mi'' itself was dropped to sixth in rank.
== Name == The title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the ''Ōkimi'' (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
These same characters of ''Ōomi'' ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in ''[[Daijō-daijin]]'', ''[[Sadaijin]]'', ''[[Udaijin]]'', ''[[Naidaijin]]'', etc.
== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}

{{Short description|Hereditary noble title in ancient Japan}} {{More citations needed|date=March 2021}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:11]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}}{{nihongo|'''''Omi'''''|臣|lead=yes}} is a hereditary [[Nobility|noble]] title (''[[kabane]]'') of ancient [[Japan]]. It was given to the descendants of the [[Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Family]] before [[Emperor Kōgen]]. Along with ''[[Muraji]]'', ''Omi'' was reserved for the head of the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. When the [[Yamato period|Yamato court]] was established, the most influential families bearing these two titles were given the title ''Ōomi'' and ''Ōmuraji'', respectively.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=Frank|title=Britannica International Encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-Britannica|year=1995|at=Omi|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
== History == The O''mi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Soga clan|Soga]]|蘇我}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]]|葛城}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Heguri]]|平群}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kose, Japan|Kose]]|巨勢}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga]]|春日}}, and the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo, Shimane|Izumo]]|出雲}}, thus making them regional chieftains in their own right.
The most powerful ''Omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the O''mi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|Ōomi}}. Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of [[Emperor Richū]], {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful O''mi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[Ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, and O''mi'' itself was dropped to sixth in rank.
== Name == The title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the ''Ōkimi'' (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
These same characters of ''Ōomi'' ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in ''[[Daijō-daijin]]'', ''[[Sadaijin]]'', ''[[Udaijin]]'', ''[[Naidaijin]]'', etc.
== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}
==See also== {{Portal|Ancient Japan}} * [[Gōzoku]]

{{Short description|Hereditary noble title in ancient Japan}} {{More citations needed|date=March 2021}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:11]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}}{{nihongo|'''''Omi'''''|臣|lead=yes}} is a hereditary [[Nobility|noble]] title (''[[kabane]]'') of ancient [[Japan]]. It was given to the descendants of the [[Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Family]] before [[Emperor Kōgen]]. Along with ''[[Muraji]]'', ''Omi'' was reserved for the head of the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. When the [[Yamato period|Yamato court]] was established, the most influential families bearing these two titles were given the title ''Ōomi'' and ''Ōmuraji'', respectively.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=Frank|title=Britannica International Encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-Britannica|year=1995|at=Omi|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
== History == The O''mi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Soga clan|Soga]]|蘇我}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]]|葛城}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Heguri]]|平群}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kose, Japan|Kose]]|巨勢}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga]]|春日}}, and the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo, Shimane|Izumo]]|出雲}}, thus making them regional chieftains in their own right.
The most powerful ''Omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the O''mi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|Ōomi}}. Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of [[Emperor Richū]], {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful O''mi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[Ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, and O''mi'' itself was dropped to sixth in rank.
== Name == The title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the ''Ōkimi'' (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
These same characters of ''Ōomi'' ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in ''[[Daijō-daijin]]'', ''[[Sadaijin]]'', ''[[Udaijin]]'', ''[[Naidaijin]]'', etc.
== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}
==See also== {{Portal|Ancient Japan}} * [[Gōzoku]]
==References== {{Reflist}}

{{draft categories| [[Category:11]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:Ancient Japan]] [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Titles]] [[Category:Kabane]] [[Category:Omi (title)]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[en:Omi (title)]] [[sn:Omi (title)]] [[ja:Omi (title)]]

{{Short description|Hereditary noble title in ancient Japan}} {{More citations needed|date=March 2021}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]]}}{{nihongo|'''''Omi'''''|臣|lead=yes}} is a hereditary [[Nobility|noble]] title (''[[kabane]]'') of ancient [[Japan]]. It was given to the descendants of the [[Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Family]] before [[Emperor Kōgen]]. Along with ''[[Muraji]]'', ''Omi'' was reserved for the head of the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. When the [[Yamato period|Yamato court]] was established, the most influential families bearing these two titles were given the title ''Ōomi'' and ''Ōmuraji'', respectively.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=Frank|title=Britannica International Encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-Britannica|year=1995|at=Omi|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
== History == The O''mi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Soga clan|Soga]]|蘇我}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]]|葛城}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Heguri]]|平群}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kose, Japan|Kose]]|巨勢}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga]]|春日}}, and the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo, Shimane|Izumo]]|出雲}}, thus making them regional chieftains in their own right.
The most powerful ''Omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the O''mi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|Ōomi}}. Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of [[Emperor Richū]], {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful O''mi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[Ason]]'', which ranked second under the new system, and O''mi'' itself was dropped to sixth in rank.
== Name == The title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the ''Ōkimi'' (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
These same characters of ''Ōomi'' ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in ''[[Daijō-daijin]]'', ''[[Sadaijin]]'', ''[[Udaijin]]'', ''[[Naidaijin]]'', etc.
== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}
==See also== {{Portal|Ancient Japan}} * [[Gōzoku]]
==References== {{Reflist}}

Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of [[Emperor Richū]], {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].

These same characters of ''Ōomi'' ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in ''[[Daijō-daijin]]'', ''[[Sadaijin]]'', ''[[Udaijin]]'', ''[[Naidaijin]]'', etc.
== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}

== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}

{{Short description|Hereditary noble title in ancient Japan}} {{More citations needed|date=March 2021}} {{Italic title|reason=}}{{nihongo|'''''Omi'''''|臣|lead=yes}} is a hereditary [[Nobility|noble]] title (''[[kabane]]'') of ancient [[Japan]]. It was given to the descendants of the [[Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Family]] before [[Emperor Kōgen]]. Along with ''[[Muraji]]'', ''Omi'' was reserved for the head of the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. When the [[Yamato period|Yamato court]] was established, the most influential families bearing these two titles were given the title ''Ōomi'' and ''Ōmuraji'', respectively.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=FRank|title=Britannica International Encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-Britannica|year=1995|at=Omi|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
== History == The O''mi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Soga clan|Soga]]|蘇我}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]]|葛城}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Heguri]]|平群}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kose, Japan|Kose]]|巨勢}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga]]|春日}}, and the {{Nihongo|[[Izumo, Shimane|Izumo]]|出雲}}, thus making them regional chieftains in their own right.
The most powerful ''Omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the O''mi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|Ōomi}}. Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of [[Emperor Richū]], {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors [[Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of [[Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful O''mi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[Ason]]'', which Ranked second under the new system, and O''mi'' itself was dropped to sixth in Rank.
== Name == The title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the ''Ōkimi'' (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
These same characters of ''Ōomi'' ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in ''[[Daijō-daijin]]'', ''[[Sadaijin]]'', ''[[Udaijin]]'', ''[[Naidaijin]]'', etc.
== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}
==See also== {{Portal|Ancient Japan}} * [[Gōzoku]]
==References== {{Reflist}}

== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[:en:Heguri no Matori|Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}

revision.text:
{{Short description|Hereditary noble title in ancient Japan}} {{More citations needed|date=March 2021}} {{Italic title|reason=}}{{nihongo|'''''Omi'''''|臣|lead=yes}} is a hereditary [[:en:Japanese nobility|noble]] title (''[[kabane]]'') of ancient [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It was given to the descendants of the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Family]] before [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]]. Along with ''[[Muraji]]'', ''Omi'' was reserved for the head of the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. When the [[Yamato period|Yamato court]] was established, the most influential families bearing these two titles were given the title ''Ōomi'' and ''Ōmuraji'', respectively.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=FRank|title=Britannica International Encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-Britannica|year=1995|at=Omi|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
== History == The O''mi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Soga clan|Soga]]|蘇我}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]]|葛城}}, the {{Nihongo|[[:en:Heguri|Heguri]]|平群}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kose, Japan|Kose]]|巨勢}}, the {{Nihongo|[[:en:Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga]]|春日}}, and the {{Nihongo|[[:en:Izumo|Izumo]]|出雲}}, thus making them regional chieftains in their own right.
The most powerful ''Omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the O''mi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|Ōomi}}. Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Richū|Emperor Richū]], {{Nihongo|[[:en:Heguri no Matori|Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[:en:Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[Soga no Emishi]] and [[Soga no Iruka]].
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful O''mi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[Ason]]'', which Ranked second under the new system, and O''mi'' itself was dropped to sixth in Rank.
== Name == The title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the ''Ōkimi'' (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
These same characters of ''Ōomi'' ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in ''[[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō-daijin]]'', ''[[:en:Sadaijin|Sadaijin]]'', ''[[Udaijin]]'', ''[[:en:Naidaijin|Naidaijin]]'', etc.
== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[:en:Heguri no Matori|Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}
==See also== {{Portal|Ancient Japan}} * [[Gōzoku]]

== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[:en:Heguri no Matori|Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Short description|Hereditary noble title in ancient Japan}} {{More citations needed|date=March 2021}} {{Italic title|reason=}}{{nihongo|'''''Omi'''''|臣|lead=yes}} is a hereditary [[:en:Japanese nobility|noble]] title (''[[kabane]]'') of ancient [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It was given to the descendants of the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Family]] before [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]]. Along with ''[[Muraji]]'', ''Omi'' was reserved for the head of the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. When the [[:en:Yamato period|Yamato court]] was established, the most influential families bearing these two titles were given the title ''Ōomi'' and ''Ōmuraji'', respectively.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=FRank|title=Britannica International Encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-Britannica|year=1995|at=Omi|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
== History == The O''mi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Soga clan|Soga]]|蘇我}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]]|葛城}}, the {{Nihongo|[[:en:Heguri|Heguri]]|平群}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kose, Japan|Kose]]|巨勢}}, the {{Nihongo|[[:en:Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga]]|春日}}, and the {{Nihongo|[[:en:Izumo|Izumo]]|出雲}}, thus making them regional chieftains in their own right.
The most powerful ''Omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the O''mi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|Ōomi}}. Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Richū|Emperor Richū]], {{Nihongo|[[:en:Heguri no Matori|Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[:en:Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] and [[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]].
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful O''mi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[Ason]]'', which Ranked second under the new system, and O''mi'' itself was dropped to sixth in Rank.
== Name == The title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the ''Ōkimi'' (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
These same characters of ''Ōomi'' ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in ''[[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō-daijin]]'', ''[[:en:Sadaijin|Sadaijin]]'', ''[[:en:Udaijin|Udaijin]]'', ''[[:en:Naidaijin|Naidaijin]]'', etc.
== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[:en:Heguri no Matori|Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}
==See also== {{Portal|Ancient Japan}} * [[Gōzoku]]

== History == The O''mi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Soga clan|Soga]]|蘇我}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]]|葛城}}, the {{Nihongo|[[:en:Heguri|Heguri]]|平群}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kose, Japan|Kose]]|巨勢}}, the {{Nihongo|[[:en:Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga]]|春日}}, and the {{Nihongo|[[:en:Izumo|Izumo]]|出雲}}, thus making them regional chieftains in their own right.
The most powerful ''Omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the O''mi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|Ōomi}}. Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Richū|Emperor Richū]], {{Nihongo|[[:en:Heguri no Matori|Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[:en:Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] and [[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]].
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful O''mi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[Ason]]'', which Ranked second under the new system, and O''mi'' itself was dropped to sixth in Rank.
== Name == The title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the ''Ōkimi'' (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
These same characters of ''Ōomi'' ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in ''[[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō-daijin]]'', ''[[:en:Sadaijin|Sadaijin]]'', ''[[:en:Udaijin|Udaijin]]'', ''[[:en:Naidaijin|Naidaijin]]'', etc.

These same characters of ''Ōomi'' ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in ''[[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō-daijin]]'', ''[[:en:Sadaijin|Sadaijin]]'', ''[[:en:Udaijin|Udaijin]]'', ''[[:en:Naidaijin|Naidaijin]]'', etc.
== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[:en:Heguri no Matori|Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}

<!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[be:Омі (тытул)]] [[fr:Omi (titre de noblesse)]] [[it:Omi]] [[ja:臣]] [[pt:Omi (título)]] [[ru:Оми (титул)]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Omi]] [[uk:Омі (титул)]] [[uz:Omi]] [[zh:臣 (姓)]] {{wikidata link|Q2574718}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[be:Омі (тытул)]][[en:Omi (title)]][[ff:Omi (title)]][[fr:Omi (titre de noblesse)]][[it:Omi]][[ja:臣]][[pt:Omi (título)]][[ru:Оми (титул)]][[simple:Omi (title)]][[uk:Омі (титул)]][[uz:Omi]][[zh:臣 (姓)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2574718]] Wikidata name: Omi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Hereditary noble title in ancient Japan}} {{More citations needed|date=March 2021}} {{Italic title|reason=}}{{nihongo|'''''Omi'''''|臣|lead=yes}} is a hereditary [[:en:Japanese nobility|noble]] title (''[[kabane]]'') of ancient [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It was given to the descendants of the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Family]] before [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]]. Along with ''[[Muraji]]'', ''Omi'' was reserved for the head of the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. When the [[:en:Yamato period|Yamato court]] was established, the most influential families bearing these two titles were given the title ''Ōomi'' and ''Ōmuraji'', respectively.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=FRank|title=Britannica International Encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-Britannica|year=1995|at=Omi|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
== History == The O''mi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Soga clan|Soga]]|蘇我}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]]|葛城}}, the {{Nihongo|[[:en:Heguri|Heguri]]|平群}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kose, Japan|Kose]]|巨勢}}, the {{Nihongo|[[:en:Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga]]|春日}}, and the {{Nihongo|[[:en:Izumo|Izumo]]|出雲}}, thus making them regional chieftains in their own right.
The most powerful ''Omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the O''mi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|Ōomi}}. Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Richū|Emperor Richū]], {{Nihongo|[[:en:Heguri no Matori|Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[:en:Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] and [[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]].
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful O''mi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[Ason]]'', which Ranked second under the new system, and O''mi'' itself was dropped to sixth in Rank.
== Name == The title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the ''Ōkimi'' (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
These same characters of ''Ōomi'' ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in ''[[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō-daijin]]'', ''[[:en:Sadaijin|Sadaijin]]'', ''[[:en:Udaijin|Udaijin]]'', ''[[:en:Naidaijin|Naidaijin]]'', etc.

[[en:Omi (title)]]
[[Category:Pages without translation templates]]
<!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[be:Омі (тытул)]] [[fr:Omi (titre de noblesse)]] [[it:Omi]] [[ja:臣]] [[pt:Omi (título)]] [[ru:Оми (титул)]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Omi]] [[uk:Омі (титул)]] [[uz:Omi]] [[zh:臣 (姓)]] {{wikidata link|Q2574718}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[be:Омі (тытул)]][[en:Omi (title)]][[ff:Omi (title)]][[fr:Omi (titre de noblesse)]][[it:Omi]][[ja:臣]][[pt:Omi (título)]][[ru:Оми (титул)]][[simple:Omi (title)]][[uk:Омі (титул)]][[uz:Omi]][[zh:臣 (姓)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[be:Омі (тытул)]][[en:Omi (title)]][[ff:Omi (title)]][[fr:Omi (titre de noblesse)]][[it:Omi]][[ja:臣]][[pt:Omi (título)]][[ru:Оми (титул)]][[simple:Omi (title)]][[uk:Омі (титул)]][[uz:Omi]][[zh:臣 (姓)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2574718]] Wikidata name: Omi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Hereditary noble title in ancient Japan}} {{More citations needed|date=March 2021}} {{Italic title|reason=}}{{nihongo|'''''Omi'''''|臣|lead=yes}} is a hereditary [[:en:Japanese nobility|noble]] title (''[[kabane]]'') of ancient [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It was given to the descendants of the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Family]] before [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]]. Along with ''[[Muraji]]'', ''Omi'' was reserved for the head of the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. When the [[:en:Yamato period|Yamato court]] was established, the most influential families bearing these two titles were given the title ''Ōomi'' and ''Ōmuraji'', respectively.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=FRank|title=Britannica International Encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-Britannica|year=1995|at=Omi|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
== History == The O''mi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Soga clan|Soga]]|蘇我}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]]|葛城}}, the {{Nihongo|[[:en:Heguri|Heguri]]|平群}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kose, Japan|Kose]]|巨勢}}, the {{Nihongo|[[:en:Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga]]|春日}}, and the {{Nihongo|[[:en:Izumo|Izumo]]|出雲}}, thus making them regional chieftains in their own right.
The most powerful ''Omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the O''mi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|Ōomi}}. Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Richū|Emperor Richū]], {{Nihongo|[[:en:Heguri no Matori|Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[:en:Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] and [[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]].
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful O''mi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[Ason]]'', which Ranked second under the new system, and O''mi'' itself was dropped to sixth in Rank.
== Name == The title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the ''Ōkimi'' (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
These same characters of ''Ōomi'' ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in ''[[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō-daijin]]'', ''[[:en:Sadaijin|Sadaijin]]'', ''[[:en:Udaijin|Udaijin]]'', ''[[:en:Naidaijin|Naidaijin]]'', etc.
== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[:en:Heguri no Matori|Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}
==See also== {{Portal|Ancient Japan}} * [[Gōzoku]]

== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[:en:Heguri no Matori|Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}

<!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[be:Омі (тытул)]] [[fr:Omi (titre de noblesse)]] [[it:Omi]] [[ja:臣]] [[pt:Omi (título)]] [[ru:Оми (титул)]] [[simple:Omi]] [[uk:Омі (титул)]] [[uz:Omi]] [[zh:臣 (姓)]] {{wikidata link|Q2574718}} [[Category:Ancient Japan]] [[Category:Asuka period]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Kofun period]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Titles]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[be:Омі (тытул)]][[en:Omi (title)]][[ff:Omi (title)]][[fr:Omi (titre de noblesse)]][[it:Omi]][[ja:臣]][[pt:Omi (título)]][[ru:Оми (титул)]][[simple:Omi (title)]][[uk:Омі (титул)]][[uz:Omi]][[zh:臣 (姓)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[be:Омі (тытул)]][[en:Omi (title)]][[ff:Omi (title)]][[fr:Omi (titre de noblesse)]][[it:Omi]][[ja:臣]][[pt:Omi (título)]][[ru:Оми (титул)]][[simple:Omi (title)]][[uk:Омі (титул)]][[uz:Omi]][[zh:臣 (姓)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[be:Омі (тытул)]][[en:Omi (title)]][[ff:Omi (title)]][[fr:Omi (titre de noblesse)]][[it:Omi]][[ja:臣]][[pt:Omi (título)]][[ru:Оми (титул)]][[simple:Omi (title)]][[uk:Омі (титул)]][[uz:Omi]][[zh:臣 (姓)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2574718]] Wikidata name: Omi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Hereditary noble title in ancient Japan}} {{More citations needed|date=March 2021}} {{Italic title|reason=}}{{nihongo|'''''Omi'''''|臣|lead=yes}} is a hereditary [[:en:Japanese nobility|noble]] title (''[[kabane]]'') of ancient [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. It was given to the descendants of the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial Family]] before [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]]. Along with ''[[Muraji]]'', ''Omi'' was reserved for the head of the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. When the [[:en:Yamato period|Yamato court]] was established, the most influential families bearing these two titles were given the title ''Ōomi'' and ''Ōmuraji'', respectively.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=FRank|title=Britannica International Encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-Britannica|year=1995|at=Omi|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
== History == The O''mi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Soga clan|Soga]]|蘇我}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]]|葛城}}, the {{Nihongo|[[:en:Heguri|Heguri]]|平群}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kose, Japan|Kose]]|巨勢}}, the {{Nihongo|[[:en:Kasuga, Fukuoka|Kasuga]]|春日}}, and the {{Nihongo|[[:en:Izumo|Izumo]]|出雲}}, thus making them regional chieftains in their own right.
The most powerful ''Omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the O''mi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|Ōomi}}. Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Richū|Emperor Richū]], {{Nihongo|[[:en:Heguri no Matori|Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Yūryaku]] and [[:en:Emperor Seinei|Seinei]], {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]], [[Soga no Umako]], [[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] and [[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]].
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful O''mi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[Ason]]'', which Ranked second under the new system, and O''mi'' itself was dropped to sixth in Rank.
== Name == The title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the ''Ōkimi'' (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
These same characters of ''Ōomi'' ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in ''[[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō-daijin]]'', ''[[:en:Sadaijin|Sadaijin]]'', ''[[:en:Udaijin|Udaijin]]'', ''[[:en:Naidaijin|Naidaijin]]'', etc.
== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|[[:en:Heguri no Matori|Heguri no Matori]]|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|[[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]]|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|[[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]]|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|[[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]]|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}

It was given to the descendants of the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=Imperial Family}} before {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|en|Emperor Kōgen|WD=Q333572|lt=Emperor Kōgen}}. Along with ''[[Muraji]]'', ''Omi'' was reserved for the head of the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. When the {{ill|Yamato period|en|Yamato period|WD=Q633881|lt=Yamato court}} was established, the most influential families bearing these two titles were given the title ''Ōomi'' and ''Ōmuraji'', respectively.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=FRank|title=Britannica International Encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-Britannica|year=1995|at=Omi|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
== History == The O''mi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Soga clan|Soga]]|蘇我}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]]|葛城}}, the {{Nihongo|[[:en:Heguri|Heguri]]|平群}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kose, Japan|Kose]]|巨勢}}, the {{Nihongo|{{ill|Kasuga, Fukuoka|en|Kasuga, Fukuoka|WD=Q47084|lt=Kasuga}}|春日}}, and the {{Nihongo|{{ill|Izumo|en|Izumo|WD=Q301580|lt=Izumo}}|出雲}}, thus making them regional chieftains in their own right.
The most powerful ''Omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the O''mi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|Ōomi}}. Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Richū|en|Emperor Richū|WD=Q329704|lt=Emperor Richū}}, {{Nihongo|{{ill|Heguri no Matori|en|Heguri no Matori|WD=Q11483500|lt=Heguri no Matori}}|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Yūryaku}} and {{ill|Emperor Seinei|en|Emperor Seinei|WD=Q357273|lt=Seinei}}, {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai}} and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: {{ill|Soga no Iname|en|Soga no Iname|WD=Q595897|lt=Soga no Iname}}, [[Soga no Umako]], {{ill|Soga no Emishi|en|Soga no Emishi|WD=Q1292368|lt=Soga no Emishi}} and {{ill|Soga no Iruka|en|Soga no Iruka|WD=Q736621|lt=Soga no Iruka}}.
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful O''mi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[Ason]]'', which Ranked second under the new system, and O''mi'' itself was dropped to sixth in Rank.
== Name == The title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the ''Ōkimi'' (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
These same characters of ''Ōomi'' ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in ''{{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=Daijō-daijin}}'', ''[[:en:Sadaijin|Sadaijin]]'', ''[[:en:Udaijin|Udaijin]]'', ''{{ill|Naidaijin|en|Naidaijin|WD=Q1185338|lt=Naidaijin}}'', etc.
== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Heguri no Matori|en|Heguri no Matori|WD=Q11483500|lt=Heguri no Matori}}|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Soga no Iname|en|Soga no Iname|WD=Q595897|lt=Soga no Iname}}|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Soga no Emishi|en|Soga no Emishi|WD=Q1292368|lt=Soga no Emishi}}|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Soga no Iruka|en|Soga no Iruka|WD=Q736621|lt=Soga no Iruka}}|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}
==See also== {{Portal|Ancient Japan}} * [[Gōzoku]]

Along with ''[[Muraji]]'', ''Omi'' was reserved for the head of the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. When the {{ill|Yamato period|en|Yamato period|WD=Q633881|lt=Yamato court}} was established, the most influential families bearing these two titles were given the title ''Ōomi'' and ''Ōmuraji'', respectively.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=FRank|title=Britannica International Encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-Britannica|year=1995|at=Omi|oclc=55231838}}</ref>

The most powerful ''Omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the O''mi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|Ōomi}}. Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Richū|en|Emperor Richū|WD=Q329704|lt=Emperor Richū}}, {{Nihongo|{{ill|Heguri no Matori|en|Heguri no Matori|WD=Q11483500|lt=Heguri no Matori}}|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Yūryaku}} and {{ill|Emperor Seinei|en|Emperor Seinei|WD=Q357273|lt=Seinei}}, {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai}} and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: {{ill|Soga no Iname|en|Soga no Iname|WD=Q595897|lt=Soga no Iname}}, [[Soga no Umako]], {{ill|Soga no Emishi|en|Soga no Emishi|WD=Q1292368|lt=Soga no Emishi}} and {{ill|Soga no Iruka|en|Soga no Iruka|WD=Q736621|lt=Soga no Iruka}}.

When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful O''mi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[Ason]]'', which Ranked second under the new system, and O''mi'' itself was dropped to sixth in Rank.
== Name == The title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the ''Ōkimi'' (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
These same characters of ''Ōomi'' ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in ''{{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=Daijō-daijin}}'', ''[[:en:Sadaijin|Sadaijin]]'', ''[[:en:Udaijin|Udaijin]]'', ''{{ill|Naidaijin|en|Naidaijin|WD=Q1185338|lt=Naidaijin}}'', etc.
== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Heguri no Matori|en|Heguri no Matori|WD=Q11483500|lt=Heguri no Matori}}|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Soga no Iname|en|Soga no Iname|WD=Q595897|lt=Soga no Iname}}|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Soga no Emishi|en|Soga no Emishi|WD=Q1292368|lt=Soga no Emishi}}|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Soga no Iruka|en|Soga no Iruka|WD=Q736621|lt=Soga no Iruka}}|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[be:Омі (тытул)]][[en:Omi (title)]][[ff:Omi (title)]][[fr:Omi (titre de noblesse)]][[it:Omi]][[ja:臣]][[pt:Omi (título)]][[ru:Оми (титул)]][[simple:Omi (title)]][[uk:Омі (титул)]][[uz:Omi]][[zh:臣 (姓)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[be:Омі (тытул)]][[en:Omi (title)]][[ff:Omi (title)]][[fr:Omi (titre de noblesse)]][[it:Omi]][[ja:臣]][[pt:Omi (título)]][[ru:Оми (титул)]][[simple:Omi (title)]][[uk:Омі (титул)]][[uz:Omi]][[zh:臣 (姓)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[be:Омі (тытул)]][[en:Omi (title)]][[ff:Omi (title)]][[fr:Omi (titre de noblesse)]][[it:Omi]][[ja:臣]][[pt:Omi (título)]][[ru:Оми (титул)]][[simple:Omi (title)]][[uk:Омі (титул)]][[uz:Omi]][[zh:臣 (姓)]]
{{Short description|Hereditary noble title in ancient Japan}} {{More citations needed|date=March 2021}} {{Italic title|reason=}}{{nihongo|'''''Omi'''''|臣|lead=yes}} is a hereditary [[:en:Japanese nobility|noble]] title (''[[kabane]]'') of ancient {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. It was given to the descendants of the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=Imperial Family}} before {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|en|Emperor Kōgen|WD=Q333572|lt=Emperor Kōgen}}. Along with ''[[Muraji]]'', ''Omi'' was reserved for the head of the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. When the {{ill|Yamato period|en|Yamato period|WD=Q633881|lt=Yamato court}} was established, the most influential families bearing these two titles were given the title ''Ōomi'' and ''Ōmuraji'', respectively.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=FRank|title=Britannica International Encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-Britannica|year=1995|at=Omi|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
== History == The O''mi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Soga clan|Soga]]|蘇我}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]]|葛城}}, the {{Nihongo|[[:en:Heguri|Heguri]]|平群}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kose, Japan|Kose]]|巨勢}}, the {{Nihongo|{{ill|Kasuga, Fukuoka|en|Kasuga, Fukuoka|WD=Q47084|lt=Kasuga}}|春日}}, and the {{Nihongo|{{ill|Izumo|en|Izumo|WD=Q301580|lt=Izumo}}|出雲}}, thus making them regional chieftains in their own right.
The most powerful ''Omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the O''mi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|Ōomi}}. Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Richū|en|Emperor Richū|WD=Q329704|lt=Emperor Richū}}, {{Nihongo|{{ill|Heguri no Matori|en|Heguri no Matori|WD=Q11483500|lt=Heguri no Matori}}|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Yūryaku}} and {{ill|Emperor Seinei|en|Emperor Seinei|WD=Q357273|lt=Seinei}}, {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai}} and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: {{ill|Soga no Iname|en|Soga no Iname|WD=Q595897|lt=Soga no Iname}}, [[Soga no Umako]], {{ill|Soga no Emishi|en|Soga no Emishi|WD=Q1292368|lt=Soga no Emishi}} and {{ill|Soga no Iruka|en|Soga no Iruka|WD=Q736621|lt=Soga no Iruka}}.
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful O''mi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[Ason]]'', which Ranked second under the new system, and O''mi'' itself was dropped to sixth in Rank.
== Name == The title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the ''Ōkimi'' (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
These same characters of ''Ōomi'' ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in ''{{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=Daijō-daijin}}'', ''[[:en:Sadaijin|Sadaijin]]'', ''[[:en:Udaijin|Udaijin]]'', ''{{ill|Naidaijin|en|Naidaijin|WD=Q1185338|lt=Naidaijin}}'', etc.
== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Heguri no Matori|en|Heguri no Matori|WD=Q11483500|lt=Heguri no Matori}}|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Soga no Iname|en|Soga no Iname|WD=Q595897|lt=Soga no Iname}}|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Soga no Emishi|en|Soga no Emishi|WD=Q1292368|lt=Soga no Emishi}}|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Soga no Iruka|en|Soga no Iruka|WD=Q736621|lt=Soga no Iruka}}|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}
==See also== {{Portal|Ancient Japan}} * [[Gōzoku]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[be:Омі (тытул)]][[en:Omi (title)]][[ff:Omi (title)]][[fr:Omi (titre de noblesse)]][[it:Omi]][[ja:臣]][[pt:Omi (título)]][[ru:Оми (титул)]][[simple:Omi (title)]][[uk:Омі (титул)]][[uz:Omi]][[zh:臣 (姓)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[be:Омі (тытул)]][[en:Omi (title)]][[ff:Omi (title)]][[fr:Omi (titre de noblesse)]][[it:Omi]][[ja:臣]][[pt:Omi (título)]][[ru:Оми (титул)]][[simple:Omi (title)]][[uk:Омі (титул)]][[uz:Omi]][[zh:臣 (姓)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[be:Омі (тытул)]][[en:Omi (title)]][[ff:Omi (title)]][[fr:Omi (titre de noblesse)]][[it:Omi]][[ja:臣]][[pt:Omi (título)]][[ru:Оми (титул)]][[simple:Omi (title)]][[uk:Омі (титул)]][[uz:Omi]][[zh:臣 (姓)]]
{{Short description|Hereditary noble title in ancient Japan}} {{More citations needed|date=March 2021}} {{Italic title|reason=}}{{nihongo|'''''Omi'''''|臣|lead=yes}} is a hereditary [[:en:Japanese nobility|noble]] title (''[[kabane]]'') of ancient {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It was given to the descendants of the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=Imperial Family|1=Imperial House of Japan}} before {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|en|Emperor Kōgen|WD=Q333572|lt=Emperor Kōgen|1=Emperor Kōgen}}. Along with ''[[Muraji]]'', ''Omi'' was reserved for the head of the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. When the {{ill|Yamato period|en|Yamato period|WD=Q633881|lt=Yamato court|1=Yamato period}} was established, the most influential families bearing these two titles were given the title ''Ōomi'' and ''Ōmuraji'', respectively.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=FRank|title=Britannica International Encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-Britannica|year=1995|at=Omi|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
== History == The O''mi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Soga clan|Soga]]|蘇我}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]]|葛城}}, the {{Nihongo|[[:en:Heguri|Heguri]]|平群}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kose, Japan|Kose]]|巨勢}}, the {{Nihongo|{{ill|Kasuga, Fukuoka|en|Kasuga, Fukuoka|WD=Q47084|lt=Kasuga|1=Kasuga, Fukuoka}}|春日}}, and the {{Nihongo|{{ill|Izumo|en|Izumo|WD=Q301580|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo}}|出雲}}, thus making them regional chieftains in their own right.
The most powerful ''Omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the O''mi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|Ōomi}}. Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Richū|en|Emperor Richū|WD=Q329704|lt=Emperor Richū|1=Emperor Richū}}, {{Nihongo|{{ill|Heguri no Matori|en|Heguri no Matori|WD=Q11483500|lt=Heguri no Matori|1=Heguri no Matori}}|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Yūryaku|1=Emperor Yūryaku}} and {{ill|Emperor Seinei|en|Emperor Seinei|WD=Q357273|lt=Seinei|1=Emperor Seinei}}, {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai|1=Emperor Keitai}} and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: {{ill|Soga no Iname|en|Soga no Iname|WD=Q595897|lt=Soga no Iname|1=Soga no Iname}}, [[Soga no Umako]], {{ill|Soga no Emishi|en|Soga no Emishi|WD=Q1292368|lt=Soga no Emishi|1=Soga no Emishi}} and {{ill|Soga no Iruka|en|Soga no Iruka|WD=Q736621|lt=Soga no Iruka|1=Soga no Iruka}}.
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful O''mi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[Ason]]'', which Ranked second under the new system, and O''mi'' itself was dropped to sixth in Rank.
== Name == The title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the ''Ōkimi'' (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
These same characters of ''Ōomi'' ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in ''{{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=Daijō-daijin|1=Daijō-daijin}}'', ''[[:en:Sadaijin|Sadaijin]]'', ''[[:en:Udaijin|Udaijin]]'', ''{{ill|Naidaijin|en|Naidaijin|WD=Q1185338|lt=Naidaijin|1=Naidaijin}}'', etc.
== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Heguri no Matori|en|Heguri no Matori|WD=Q11483500|lt=Heguri no Matori|1=Heguri no Matori}}|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Soga no Iname|en|Soga no Iname|WD=Q595897|lt=Soga no Iname|1=Soga no Iname}}|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Soga no Emishi|en|Soga no Emishi|WD=Q1292368|lt=Soga no Emishi|1=Soga no Emishi}}|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Soga no Iruka|en|Soga no Iruka|WD=Q736621|lt=Soga no Iruka|1=Soga no Iruka}}|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}
==See also== {{Portal|Ancient Japan}} * [[Gōzoku]]

Along with ''[[Muraji]]'', ''Omi'' was reserved for the head of the most powerful clans during the [[Kofun period]]. When the {{ill|Yamato period|en|Yamato period|WD=Q633881|lt=Yamato court|1=Yamato period}} was established, the most influential families bearing these two titles were given the title ''Ōomi'' and ''Ōmuraji'', respectively.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Gibney|first=FRank|title=Britannica International Encyclopaedia|publisher=TBS-Britannica|year=1995|at=Omi|oclc=55231838}}</ref>
== History == The O''mi'' clans generally took their names from the geographic location from which they originated, such as the {{Nihongo|[[Soga clan|Soga]]|蘇我}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Katsuragi clan|Katsuragi]]|葛城}}, the {{Nihongo|[[:en:Heguri|Heguri]]|平群}}, the {{Nihongo|[[Kose, Japan|Kose]]|巨勢}}, the {{Nihongo|{{ill|Kasuga, Fukuoka|en|Kasuga, Fukuoka|WD=Q47084|lt=Kasuga|1=Kasuga, Fukuoka}}|春日}}, and the {{Nihongo|{{ill|Izumo|en|Izumo|WD=Q301580|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo}}|出雲}}, thus making them regional chieftains in their own right.
The most powerful ''Omi'' added the prefix {{Nihongo3||大|ō|lit. "great"}} to the O''mi'' title, and were referred to as {{Nihongo3||大臣|Ōomi}}. Examples of ''Ōomi'' mentioned in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' included {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Richū|en|Emperor Richū|WD=Q329704|lt=Emperor Richū|1=Emperor Richū}}, {{Nihongo|{{ill|Heguri no Matori|en|Heguri no Matori|WD=Q11483500|lt=Heguri no Matori|1=Heguri no Matori}}|平群馬鳥}} during the reign of Emperors {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Yūryaku|1=Emperor Yūryaku}} and {{ill|Emperor Seinei|en|Emperor Seinei|WD=Q357273|lt=Seinei|1=Emperor Seinei}}, {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai|1=Emperor Keitai}} and the four generations of [[Soga clan|Sogas]] who dominated the title during the 6th and 7th centuries: {{ill|Soga no Iname|en|Soga no Iname|WD=Q595897|lt=Soga no Iname|1=Soga no Iname}}, [[Soga no Umako]], {{ill|Soga no Emishi|en|Soga no Emishi|WD=Q1292368|lt=Soga no Emishi|1=Soga no Emishi}} and {{ill|Soga no Iruka|en|Soga no Iruka|WD=Q736621|lt=Soga no Iruka|1=Soga no Iruka}}.
When the ''kabane'' system was reformed into the [[kabane|eight kabane]] system in 684 following a series of coup attempts, the powerful O''mi'' of the time were given the ''kabane'' of ''[[Ason]]'', which Ranked second under the new system, and O''mi'' itself was dropped to sixth in Rank.
== Name == The title denoted supremacy within the court, with titular power belonging to the ''Ōkimi'' (later denoted Emperor), whether or not he actually held power.
These same characters of ''Ōomi'' ({{Nihongo2|大臣}}) are pronounced ''Daijin'' to refer to titles beyond 670 A.D. in ''{{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=Daijō-daijin|1=Daijō-daijin}}'', ''[[:en:Sadaijin|Sadaijin]]'', ''[[:en:Udaijin|Udaijin]]'', ''{{ill|Naidaijin|en|Naidaijin|WD=Q1185338|lt=Naidaijin|1=Naidaijin}}'', etc.
== List of ''Ōomi'' == * {{Nihongo|[[Takenouchi no Sukune]]|武内宿禰}} (84–?) * {{Nihongo|Wanihifure Omi no mikoto|和邇日触}} * {{Nihongo|Katsuragi no Tsubura|葛城円}} (?–456) * {{Nihongo|Mononobe no Omae|物部小前}} (?–?) * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Heguri no Matori|en|Heguri no Matori|WD=Q11483500|lt=Heguri no Matori|1=Heguri no Matori}}|平群馬鳥}} (?–498) * {{Nihongo|Kose no Ohito|許勢男人}} (?–529) * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Soga no Iname|en|Soga no Iname|WD=Q595897|lt=Soga no Iname|1=Soga no Iname}}|蘇我稲目}} (<abbr>c.</abbr> 506–570) * {{Nihongo|[[Soga no Umako]]|蘇我馬子}} (551–626) * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Soga no Emishi|en|Soga no Emishi|WD=Q1292368|lt=Soga no Emishi|1=Soga no Emishi}}|蘇我蝦夷}} (587–645) * {{Nihongo|{{ill|Soga no Iruka|en|Soga no Iruka|WD=Q736621|lt=Soga no Iruka|1=Soga no Iruka}}|蘇我入鹿}} (?–645)
== In popular culture == The name "Omi" holds a superior power in "HvH".{{Clarify|date=March 2021}}{{Citation needed|date=March 2021}}
